real_questions: What's your operating range for capital?
Are there any capital raises assumed in your plan?
common_pitfalls: Issuers premise capital release plans on the current regulatory framework even though requirements are about to be updated. S&P will probe the contingency if updated requirements constrain the planned release pace.
strong_answer_markers: Comes prepared with an explicit 18–24 month capital view and a long-term run-rate capital target.
agency_intel: S&P's capital anchor is the risk-adjusted capital (RAC) ratio — their own evaluation of capital, with a numerator most comparable to Tier 1 and a denominator that is S&P's own risk-weighted asset calculation, separate from the regulatory one. The RAC ratio is what S&P is most focused on when rating banks.
Operational risk sits inside the denominator of S&P's RAC ratio, so operational risk gets more focus at S&P than at the other agencies.
S&P is more likely than the other agencies to look across to competitors when assigning the rating — peer positioning matters.

S&P sees room for capital releases — improvements in capital ratios, lower stress capital buffers, and declines in unrealized losses have created the room. However, S&P cautions that the extent only becomes clearer once regulators update capital requirements.
